Such an act was seen by Buenos Aires as a declaration of war, which prompted a Porteńo invasion of the Argentine Confederation. The invasion resulted in the Battle of Cepeda, which was a Federal victory, leaving the path to Buenos Aires open. 

Now, Urquiza marches towards Buenos Aires. We are forced to munster a new army if we want to have any chance to not be forcefully integrated into the Confederation.

\n\nWith another conflict with the Argentine Confederation ending in a stalemate, it is about time we think about our future. For how long will we wage war against the Federalists? Despite not being able to defeat the Confederation, we still hold enough leverage to have them accept the definitive secession of Buenos Aires from Argentina, letting us follow our future in peace, free from their rule once and for all.

\n\nWith another conflict with the Argentine Confederation ending in a stalemate, the State of Buenos Aires seems to finally have decided that enough has enough and declared formal secession from Argentina. Seeing as we were unable to defeat them once and for all, we have no option but to accept that Buenos Aires is out of our reach and that we are better off learning to live without them as part of the Argentine Confederation.
A Capital for the Confederation

Until now, Paraná has been the Federal capital of the Argentine Confederation ever since Rosas was deposed and Buenos Aires broke away from us. But now, with the matter of Buenos Aires dealt with, it is finally time we decide on a definitive capital for the Argentine Confederation, lest the savages in Buenos Aires find a way to let their city be our capital once again.

\n\nWe can't deny that whichever city takes the place as the official capital of the Confederation has big shoes to fill, especially with how it will have to fight to stay more influential than Buenos Aires, while at the same time not being overbearing on the provinces. One of the options, of course, is to keep the capital in Paraná, which would save on the work of moving our administration anywhere. One popular option is to move the capital to Rosário, which is better suited to be the capital of the nation, or even moving the capital to Tucumán, to appeal to the Interior. On the other hand, some people argue that we would need a newly-built city to serve as the capital to truly eclipse Buenos Aires as our administrative center!

\n\nHaving decided to build a new city from the ground up, there are a few options of were to do it. We could build a new city in the La Plata River, bearing the same name—La Plata—or even a city on the Paraná River—Federación. Another option, far from the La Plata River would be a city in the south—Belgrano. And last, but not lest, if we were to stay away from the coast, our final option would somewhere in the interior—the city of Unión.
Partido Federal (Apostólicos)
Partido Federal (Doctrinarios)
Positivist Oligarchy

Our country is currently ruled by a governmental and intellectual elite of positivist oligarchs that are bent on staying on power by any means necessary—while keeping the guise of a democratic government, of course.

After the presidencies of Mitre, Sarmiento and Avellaneda, Júlio Argentino Roca—as the 4th President of the Argentine Republic—set the stage for the political climate in Argentina for the next few decades, giving way for the Generation of '80, the governing and intellectual elite that dominated Argentinian politics under the National Autonomist Party for well over 30 years. Under the uncontested rule of the National Autonomist Party, Argentina found itself influenced by the positivist ideas of Comte, with governance being carried with optimism and the idea that the country would grow in every aspect. 

The period was also marked by the crackdown on provincial autonomy, leading to provicial identities fading away with time and the country becoming more centralized.

After the fall of the Confederation, Mitre had an easy time subjugating the Interior—apart from Mendoza, most provinces fell without putting up much of a fight. But that didn't mean that Federalism was quite dead in the Interior—far from it. The Revolution of the Colorados was the last Federalist revolt in the Interior, happening right in the middle of the Paraguayan War, with the provinces of Mendoza, San Luis, San Juan and La Rioja falling to the revolt, with it also finding some support in provinces such as Catamarca, Cordoba, Corrientes and Santa Fé. 

To our luck, Urquiza—the Federalist Leader and Governor of Entre Rios—refrained from showing support to the revolt, despite the plea from the Federalist leaders, else we would risk finding ourselves returning to Federalist rule.

With Urquiza's silence in face of the Federalist revolt in the Interior, one of his old generals—Ricardo Ramón López Jordán—would turn against him, slowly plotting his revenge. On the 11th of April Jordán would revolt with a botched attempt to arrest Urquiza, which would result in the death of the Federalist leader. After that Jordán would assume as Governor of Entre Rios, but President Sarmiento would see this as an challenge to his authority, prompting him to send a troop of veterans to Entre Rios, expelling Jordan from the province. 

Jordán would continue to clash with the Unitarian government until his arrest in the 16th of December 1876. He would later escape prision and exile himself in Montevideo, until returning in 1888 after being given amnesty, only to be assassinated in 1889. After the end of his revolt, Federalism would be dead, with its last remaining followers assimilating into Alsina's Autonomist Party.
Generación del '80

It was a quiet morning in Buenos Aires. Once distant from the city center was Palermo, a vicious swamp which was on San Benito de Palermo was built on, a home fitting for a gentleman—Juan Manuel de Rosas. He slept always ready and close to his horse—in case death were to come to him at night—through its treacherous hands. But no amount of paranoia could save him from time and illness. Death would come to Rosas suddenly. A common cold would be too much for the 83 year old, leading to his death from pneumonia. 

Now, his son, Juan Bautista de Rosas, is meant to follow on his father's footsteps and guide the Argentine Confederation into the future. Will he follow on his father's footsteps? Or will he finally fold and give freedom to Argentina?

The Battle of Caseros has brought new life to the Argentine political landscape, the discussions in Santa Fé became a breeding ground for political thought aimed at deciding the future of the Argentine Confederation. Recently, with the writings of Alberdi taking the Constitutient Assembly by storm, the Cuzco Club decided it was time to act before further discussions were made. 

Representatives from the Cuzco Club have opened today's session in the Constitutient Assembly by proposing a forgotten idea—turning Argentina into a kingdom with an Inca as its monarch. The idea is far from new with it being suggested in the 1816 Council of Tucumán by Belgrano and supported by San Martín, at the time suggesting that a monarchy would find easier recognition by the European monarchs.

\n\nWhen a constitution was finally implemented, no one thought that Rosas would willingly give his power like that. The Restorer of Laws was always quick to steer politics into his interests, believing himself to be a benevolent dictator to his people. Something seems to really have changed. Rosas has officially stepped down for power as 1st President of the Argentine Confederation, giving way to a new politician who will be sure to be influenced by him to some degree. Regardless, this still dawns a new day for the Argentine Confederation, a day many waited for with for a long time.

\n\nWith Rosas gone it seems that many of his old allies feared that Juan wouldn't be as competent or friendly as his father was and such—together with an influx of old Unitarians seeing Rosas' death as the opportunity to return to Buenos Aires—have organized a revolt against Juan in Buenos Aires, expelling the Federal government from the city and forcing us to take action against our own capital.

\n\nWe would be lying if we didn't say the news of Rosas' death didn't bring us joy to some degree. Sadly, we now learn that his death brough upon the Confederation a revolt by the Unitarians, leading to the new Federal government having to declare war on the capital in Buenos Aires. While we can't help but feel that is the natural outcome of giving that much power to Buenos Aires, maybe we could make the most of the situation and return to the Confederation, leading it to victory against the Unitarians one last time.

\n\nWe would be lying if we didn't say the news of Rosas' death didn't bring us joy to some degree. Better yet, we now learn that his death brough upon the Confederation a revolt by the Unitarians, leading to the new Federal government having to declare war on the capital in Buenos Aires. Maybe we could make the most of the situation and help Buenos Aires overthrow Rosas.

\n\nOur current situation has attracted the attention of $FROMCOUNTRY$, which after breaking off from the Confederation long ago seems to be interested in helping us against the Unitarians, as long as they can rule the Confederation afterwards, it seems.

\n\nOur current situation has attracted the attention of $FROMCOUNTRY$, which after breaking off from the Confederation long ago seems to be interested in reforming the Confederation under their rule, declaring war against Buenos Aires and the rest of Argentina.
\n\nDespite our best intentions, it would seem the $FROMCOUNTRY$ wasn't very interested in letting us running the Confederation, even if it means getting our help.
\n\nBuenos Aires has finally been pacified. We have put to rest the last of Unitarian resistance to our rule and we are finally free to consolidate our rule over Argentina.

\n\nThe separation of the Litoral from Argentina was controversial, for sure. But it was a necessary measure against Rosas Totalitarianism. Now, with Rosas gone there is nothing in the way of reunifying our two nations, especially if the democratic advancements from the Litoral are implemented in the rest of the country.

With the Rosas' triumph against all of Argentina's enemies, Argentina has consolidated its position as an independent South American power. Despite that, now that Rosas is gone, we can't help but examine what did he left behind 

Will Rosism survive without Rosas? Or it is time that we return to our Federalist ideals now that the Restorer of Laws is gone?
The South Peruvian Protectorate

§RGain 2.5-5 infamy, depending on if you or your puppet own Cusco!§! Despite the shifting economic and political developments since independence, South Peru stills remains connected to Bolivia in one way or another. This connection has long been the a point of contention between Peru and Bolivia, even leading to Santa Cruz's Peru-Bolivian Confederation at one point, but before that, the connection between South Peru and Bolivia—in face of the independence war and concerns over Lima's stability—pushed one of our heroes, San Martín, into considering the possibility of an autonomous South Peruvian protectorate, under Platine—or Chilean—supervision. While the days of our independence war are long over, with our growing dominance over South Andean affair, we could try to use such plans to our advantage and detach South Peru from Lima, strengthening its connections to old Bolivia, allowing us to expand our great state and placate the people of Alto Peru.
What do you mean the Foreign Affairs Ministry is calling it "Provincia del Cuzco"

With the creation of our newly-established Protectorate of Cusco, our influence over South Peru is secure. On paper, we are to decide its fate on a later date, but we aren't sure if everyone in our government is very keen on having South Peru rejoining Peru or even becoming its own republic again. Some of the documents directed at the protectorate already refer to Cusco as a province, instead of a protectorate, worrying some in our government which are not interested in incorporating South Peru at all. 

As of now, while some in our government try to assure its protectorate status, others are sure to slowly plant the idea of South Peru as just another province, so it can be eventually be made an integral part of our nation.

After "liberating" South Peru, we established a 5-year protectorate to help the region to function independently from Lima and today, that time is finally up. For some, the date comes not a day too early, with some in Cusco having opposed our rule ever since official documents started refering to ther region as "Provincia del Cuzco". Speaking of which, such incidents only became more frequent as time went on, with most documents at this point refering to the region as such. At this point, many think that integration into our nation is only a matter of time or if it hasn't already happened. 

Still, some fear still fear that South Peru will be freed soon and such, the government of Alto Peru has asked us to transfer some of protectorate's territory, as compensation for our protection and to protect the Alto Peruvian economy.
José de San Martín

Despite his old age, the return of San Martín was met with joy by most of the Argentine Confederation. The hero has since returned to military service and even if his role is limited due to his advancing age, his mere presence is enough to raise our morale and increase our prestige in the region!

\n\nAfter the death of his wife, José Francisco de San Martín y Matorras—father of the nation and the hero of Argentine, Chilean and Peruvian independence—sailed out of South America, taking his daughter whom was to study in Europe. Despite that, the man has kept a close eye on Argentine politics, to the point of admiring Rosas for putting an end to the civil war that plagued the country for so long, leading to the two men exchange friendly letters between one another regularly. Today, a special letter came into Rosas' office. With news of the French Blockade of La Plata San Martín has officially offered his services to Rosas and the Argentine Confederation. While we could hardly refuse such an offer, his health has declined significantly ever since the days of the independence war, with his role in our military probably being severy limited if he were to return.

When San Martín came back to Argentina, he would soon find himself fighting against not only the Anglo-French Blockade, but also the Unitarians acting as their allies on the mainland. With the defeat of the Unitarians, the Blockade was lifted and Argentina stood triumphant against the European powers, much to the delight of San Martin. 

With the worst of the fighting over and driven blind with age, San Martín resigned from the army once and for all, quietly retiring to Buenos Aires where he would spend his last days, dying shortly after in the company of his daughter and grandchildren. His burial was quick and without much cerimony in Buenos Aires, as per his will. 

Rosas, on the other hand, arranged for a small ceremony in his honor at the heart of Buenos Aires in which the dictator was given San Martín's saber and read some of San Martín's last words to him, thanking Rosas for defending the honor of republic against foreign powers.

Belgrano and Artigas, both heroes of the Argentine Confederation, having fought for its freedom against the Spanish Crown and Porteńo centralism respectively. None of them, however, were as great as José Francisco de San Martín y Matorras, not even the Father of Federalism—Artigas—or the tragic hero of independence—Belgrano. Having liberated half a continent, his deeds go beyond our borders, with his liberation of Chile and Peru leaving him as one of, if not, the greatest liberator in South America. 

With the political situation of the Confederation stabilizing after so long we are free to finally ponder about San Martín's role as one of our hero and declare him "Father of the Fatherland", securing his legacy besides Artigas and Belgrano and using his sympathies—and later support—towards federalism to strengthen the founding principles.

Despite being a hero to Chile and Peru, San Martín remains a controversial figure in Argentina. The Federalists were quick to acclaim him as a national hero, having being favored by him since the start of the Argentine Civil Wars, despite his commitment to not get involved in our squabbles. Such commitment was what made the Unitarian Party and its political successors ressent him, with his refusal to aid the Supreme Directory against the Federalists. 

But that changes now, with ex-president Bartolomé Mitre publishing "Historia de San Martín y de la emancipación sudamericana", a (biased) biography of San Martín, leading to his universal acclaim as a South American liberator and creation of several innacuracies to portray San Martín as a Unitarian hero, turning his figure into a convenient to the current Argentine Republic.

José Francisco de San Martín y Matorras died on the 17th of August 1850, in the small town of Boulogne in northern France. The body of the Father of the Fatherland was to be returned to Argentina and to be burried in Buenos Aires as per his will, but—due to Urquiza's revolt—his return was delayed and later, with Buenos Aires being dominated by Unitarians and seceding from Argentina, the return of his body was delayed indeindefinitely. 

Now, with the political situation in Argentina finally stabilizing and the position of San Martín as a hero reaffirmed, there are finally favorable conditions for his body is to head back to Argentina once and for all, with his remains being burried in Buenos Aires Metropolitan Cathedral, where he may rest once and for all.
The Legacy of San Martín

§RGain 5 infamy§! Argentina has many heros, but they all pale in comparison to San Martín. The legacy of the Father of the Fatherland is felt even beyond our borders, with our hero even being a revered figure in Chile and Peru, besides O'Higgins and Bolívar respectively. \n\nNow that we became a stable great power in the Southern Cone, it is only natural we consider how his legacy could be used to influence the other nations on which he is celebrated.

"My sword has not come to dominate Chile, but to liberate it."

Such were the words of San Martín upon his refusal of power in Chile following its liberation. This short sentence has been the thorn on the side of chavunist intelligentsia. With our recent rise to power and stabilization, there are some in our government that wish for us to take the role of hegemon in South America, using the legacy of San Martín to justify incursions into Chile, Peru and even Ecuador, creating a new union between our nations. That would go against everything that San Martín stood for, and that fact isn't lost on us, being a major point by more moderate minds. Some try to say that San Martín would want such a union between the countries he liberated, while others outright say that his wishes hardly matter for our ambitions.

\n\nWhile San Martín poses no threat to Rosas' regime, his return undoubtedly rallied some people around him, soldiers and intellectuals alike are fascinated by his figure, with the hero being closer to a celebrity than a general these days. But recently, a special group of people rallied around him, "The Cuzco Club", a group of monarchist intellectuals which took special interest in Belgrano's 1816 Inca Monarchy Plan in the Congress of Tucumán, which San Martín and the Federalists supported. While the plan was proposed as to more easily gather support from European powers, with our recent conflicts against the French and the English, some can't help but wonder if a monarchy still wouldn't help us to drive away the hateful eyes of our European adversaries.
Local Economic Upturn

\n\nWith our reintegration of Potosí—whether we annex the rest of Alto Peru or not—the Interior provinces of Argentina have seen a significant economic upturn. These provinces—before our independence—had their economies and trade oriented towards Potosí, with an independent Bolivia posing a significant setback to their economy and severely hurting the Federalist position in the region. Now, we are happy to notice that—with the reintegration of Potosí—the Interior has reoriented their trade back towards Potosí, leading to a significant flourish of the local economies due to old routes being brought back, undoing the economic catastrophe caused by Bolivian independence and attracting settlers to the region.

Chile has been under our control for a while even before our policy shifted towards persuing a union between our nations. Once we had our trusted men ruling in Santiago, Chile was organized into a group of provinces which enjoy special autonomy, with our rule being mediated through Santiago. Now, with our new ambitions declared, we called for a meeting with the Chilean governors in Maipú to discuss the future of Chile until the union with Peru is formalized. The main question posed by the Chilean governors is if Chile will enjoy of its special autonomy until such a union comes to life, or if Chilean integration will be conducted as soon as possible. 

Either way, our mission in Chile for the following years is rooting out any remaining resistance to our rule and fostering a sense of brotherhood between our two nations...
The Sister Republic Across the Andes

§RGain 7.5 infamy§! With the failure of the Army of the North, San Martín determined that securing Chilean independence would be pivotal to secure Platine independence in the face of the failures in Alto Peru. Not only he would be proven right, but his plans would come to fruition with the Army of the Andes and the Chilean Declaration of Independence following the Battle of Maipú. While our relations were friendly—with our armies fighting together against the Peru-Bolivian Confederation—disputes would slowly surface over our borders in Patagonia and the Andes, leading to a falling out between our two nations. Our newly announced ambitions served only to further grow the rift between our nations, leaving us no choice but to turn their worse fears into reality and leave clear our intention to force them into our beautiful union.

\n\nOur recent ambitions on Chile recently bore fruit, with Chile coming swiftly under our control. While the Andes may prove a obstacle in the integration of Chile, our main priority now will be to crush the remaining resistance and foster a sense of brotherhood between our nations. While this surely will be a monumental task, there is still the question if we will relegate Chile's running to local governors or if we are to govern it directly across from the Andes.

\n\nWith the integration of Alto Perú, we once again have full control over Potosí and its mine. Potosí not only was once the economic center of the region, rivaling Buenos Aires and Lima, but it also was the site of one of the most pretigious coin mints in the Americas—which coined Argentina's first coins. Of course, after our independence, we took our minting operations away from the city, but now that is once again in our hands we have to decided if we are to once again have it as our national mint or if we are to let Potosí fade into the the annals of history.
National Mint
Dominate Lima

§RGain 5-15 infamy§! When San Martín liberated Peru, he worried that the Liman elites would impose their will on the rest of the country and, whether this happened or not, we used it to justify our take over of South Peru and—now that South Peru has been dealt with—our next objetive lies in completely dominating Lima and the rest of Peru, with the hopes of preparing it for a future union between our nations.

\n\nAs news of our victory spread across the Americas, many of the exiled Unitarians hiding in Chile and Montevideo can finally return to Argentina, leading to the return of many of the Unitarian leaders that were driven into exile by the Federalists victories over Buenos Aires—including the 1st President of Argentina, Bernadino Rivadavia.

Despite being able to reconquer Buenos Aires by starting a revolt in the countryside, the Interior is far from our support base. Now that we returned to Buenos Aires, it became clear that the Interior is a savage and backwards land, with our newly-appointed governors having a hard time ruling over anything west of Córdoba. 

If we are to have true control over Argentina, we will have to have put some work into not only integrating the Interior, but subduing any resistance to our rule in the region, lest the "weak" and unruly Interior leads to our downfall once again.
The Hostile Interior
Subdue the Interior

To Unitarism, the Interior is a hassle—a savage land of uncivilized natives, unruly gauchos and despotic caudillos. Our control over the Interior is weak, with our appointed governors west of Córdoba having a hard time not being toppled by local rivals and Federalist revolts. While we can keep supressing the Interior, it is clear that having to do so only depletes our resources and leaves us open to being toppled by the Federalists once again. \n\nIf we are to finally subdue the Savage Interior, we need to not only modernize our administration of the territory, but also reform our military structure so charismatic tyrants can't threaten our governors with the help of militant gauchos anymore.
Partido Unitario (Rivadavistas)
Partido Unitario (Liberales)
Partido Federal

\n\nWhile the Interior is a natural enemy of our Unitarian ideology, no region is more outrageous than Mesopotamia. Ever since the rise of José Gervasio Artigas anything beyond the Paraná River sat firmly under the Federalist influence and our recent victories haven't changed that. While at first we were able to depose the governors of Entre Rios and Corrientes, both provinces are now under a violent guerrilla campaign carried out by the Federalists with native support. We hope our governors in the region can hold on by themselves, as we aren't in the position to pacify the region ourselves...

\n\nThe Unitarian Party was never agaisnt letting Argentina become a democratic republic with free elections, but the dominant stance was that a temporary dictatorship would be necessary as we properly build Argentina as an independent state. While now we have a vocal minority of liberals urging us to liberalize at once, the Rivadavian position of keeping a dictatorship for the time being reigns supreme, seeing as in the months following our return to Buenos Aires it became clear that we still have our fair share of enemies across the country. If we were to liberalize now, we would be showing weakness and handing the country over to those savage Federalists!

\n\nDespite being at odds with most of the country and having a hard time gathering support among the lower classes, the Unitarian regime over Argentina survived. Sadly, the same couldn't be said for the Unitarian Party as a whole. While Unitarian ideals are meant to dominate Argentina, as the old guard of ideological hardliners slowly faded into irrelevancy, Rivadavian Unitarism died, giving way for a new generation of Unitarists, desilusioned with the old ways of the party. Ever since the start of the decade new factions have been rising up in the party and now, without the old guard to keep the party together, the Unitarian Party has officially dissolved giving way to its successor—the Liberal Party.
Invest in the Southern Andes

With our recent conquest of Alto Peru, we are met with a challenge. Alto Peru—nowdays known as Bolivia—was always meant to be part of Argentina, but due to the Army of the North's failture to liberate the region, the region would end up becoming its own nation which—much to our dismay—ended up enacting a policy of trying to distance itself from us. While we now control Alto Peru for the first time since our independence, the damage is already done, as we now need to invest in the the local population so that they see that we are kin and meant to fight side-by-side as brothers for $COUNTRY$!
Accept South Andeans

Ever since the first days of our independence, many had a vision of Alto Peru—also known as Bolivia—being part of our nation. Sadly, now this vision has come into fruition, it became clear the the people of Alto Peru hardly have accepted the fact that they are now a part of our nation. Luckily—with some nice gestures showing our commitment to them—we can reintegrate them and teach their place in our great nation.
Revive 1816 Inca Plan

§RGain 24.99 infamy§! In 1816, in the wake of Argentinian Independence, Manuel Belgrano proposed that not only La Plata, Chile and Peru should unite under single nation, but also that—to gather recognition from the European powers—such a nation should crown a monarch, an Inca monarch to be more precise. Now that we went through with such plans to crown an Inca King, it is about time that we seize our position as a rising power to declare the need to realize the 1816 Inca Plan and unite most of Hispanic South America under our great monarchy!

\n\nA new trend started spreading among muslims across Russia, with muslim intellectuals promoting the creating of "New Method Schools" in direct opposition to traditionalist schools for the Muslim populations of our nation. With it came the a new class of Muslim reformers, the Jadids, whose ideas quickly spread across the Empire, from its first steps in Crimea to the depths of Central Asia. In general, the Jadid Movement main focus was on reforming the education of Muslims across the Russian Empire—including the education of women—and promoting a modernization Islamic social and cultural life. In general, the Jadidist were held in a higher regard by the Russian population, seeing them as a good alternative to the "extremists" of the Ulama and Islamic Clergy in general.

\n\nFollowing the proliferation of the Jadid Movement across the Russian Empire, the Young Khivans political movement was formed. While at first the movement restricted itself to cultural and educational endeavors—as did much of the Jadid Movement—it would also slowly promote the liberalization of the Khanate of Khiva, eventually managing to get Isfandiyar Khan to establish a short-lived constitutional monarchy, even if such an arrangement would soon be undone and Khiva would once again become a backwards absolute monarchy before finally dissolving into the Khorezm People's Soviet Republic.

\n\nFollowing the proliferation of the Jadid Movement across the Russian Empire, the Young Bukharans society was formed. While at first the movement restricted itself to modernizing Bukhara—as did much of the Jadid Movement—it soon would find itself in the middle of the of the Russian Civil War, siding with the Bolshevik government and becoming the basis for the first government of the Bukharan People's Soviet Republic. Sadly, most of its members would end up purged after 1936.
Force Isfandiyar Khan to publish the Young Khivans Manifesto

With the recent wave of revolts and revolutionary movements across Central Asia and especially Khiva, the Young Khivans have the perfect opportunity to force Isfandiyar Khan into signing a manifesto establishing a legislative Majlis and put prominent Young Khivan Bobohun Salimov as its chairman. Despite the Khan being bound to accept, as a conservative we know that he won't be happy with the arrangement and will look for a way of undermining the Young Khivans to recover his power.

\n\nDespite being able to get concessions from Isfandiyar Khan, the Young Khivans would instantly see themselves at odds with the Khan, with him being able to exploit his position of ruler of Khiva to hinder the modernization efforts of the Majlis. Now, the khan plans to depose and persecute the members of the Young Khivan movement with the help of the Russian Cossacks. The situation is tense, but there may be a chance for the Young Khivans to put an end to the Khan's tyranny once and for all...

\n\nBeing one of our newly-adquired territories, Cusco sits far away from our usual seat of power. Despite that, there recently were talks about maybe moving our administrative capital to the region, as the city once was planned to be the capital of a union between Peru and La Plata. While it would be a costly and cumbersome move, it would also greatly help us manage our new Andine holdings without much problems...

President Manuel Oribe from Uruguay was deposed by the Colorado Party and fled the country. While he planned on seeking refuge on Buenos Aires at first, Oribe was soon turned away by the unsympathetic Unitarian government, which feared the Blanco leader would end up leading to instability, considering his ideological similiarities with the Argentine Federal Party. 

Instead, he arrived earlier this morning in the port of Rio de Janeiro, with a few interested observers going out to meet him and hear stories about his experiences. While we don't see Oribe and threat, considering Rivera's tied to the Unitarians in Argentina and revolutionaries from Rio Grande do Sul, maybe having him at our disposal would be useful in the future.

\n\nDespite being able to restore our hold over most of Rio Grande do Sul, the local revolutionaries are still active in the state, carrying out a guerrilla campaign with support from the Uruguyan Colorados. Now, with the defeat of the Liberals in Săo Paulo and Minas Gerais, the Rio-grandense Revolutionaries intensified their activities, trying to make the most out of the wave of new recruits coming from the failed revolts. While the situation is under control now, with their new allies and Uruguayan support, Rio Grande do Sul could once again become a problem in the future. It may be a good idea to consider dispatching a punitive expedition into Uruguay to rout out the remaining revolutionary resistance.

When Rivera was removed from the head of the army by Oribe, the revolutionaries in Rio Grande do Sul helped Rivera engage in a military campaign to oust Oribe from power and return to the Uruguayan presidency. Sadly, the war against Brazil hasn't gone as well for the Rio-grandense people, with their struggle being reduced to a guerrilla movement that we were happy to support. 

Recently, we were informed that Brazil is considering dispatching a punitive expedition against us to quell any Rio-grandense resistance and support we are currently offering them. While we maybe could defuse the situation, with ex-oresident Manuel Oribe residing in Brazil, it is hard to believe that he wouldn't use this opportunity to gather Brazilian support to return to Uruguay. Maybe we reach out for our old contacts in Buenos Aires to support us in this time of need...

\n\nThings seem tense in the other side of the Uruguay River as Brazil has been fighting against revolutionaries in the Rio Grande do Sul Province for what feels like a decade now. Those revolutionaries have long been aided by the Uruguyan president Fructuoso Rivera and that fact has hardly gone past Brazil unnoticed. Rivera has recently came to Buenos Aires, fearing that Brazil will soon dispatch a punitive expedition against him to install Oribe as president and end any support to the Rio-grandense cause. While we would rather let a caudillo like him suffer for the consequences of his actions, not only Rivera's ideology is somewhat allignes to ours but Oribe has lots of connections to the Federal Party, which could cause us problems in the future.

\n\nThe Unitarians in Buenos Aires—despite their usual distaste for caudillos—have agreed to help us in the event of a future Brazilian invasion. While their help will be useful, we are also looking into getting help from revolutionaries such as a certain Giuseppe Garibaldi—currently residing in Montevideo with his wife, after losing faith in the Farroupilha cause...

\n\nThe Unitarians in Buenos Aires—showing their usual distaste for caudillos—have refused to help us in the event of a future Brazilian invasion, saying that this a grave that Rivera dug himself. While we can't deny that our support to the Farrapos put us in this situation, we would be lying if we said we weren't expecting their backing on this matter. Not all is lost, however, with the exiled Italian Redshirts offering their support under their leader Giuseppe Garibaldi.

\n\nManuel Oribe—the exiled ex-president of Uruguay currently residing in Rio de Janeiro—hearing of our plans to dispatch a punitive expedition to Uruguay came to us with a proposal. Oribe proposes that we enjoy his support in our campaign against Rivera and the Farrapos, but—in return—he desires to be restored as President of Uruguay. We can't deny that such an offer serves us perfectly, as using the restoration of Oribe as a casus belli, not only would we have legitimacy to wage our war but we also would get support from pretty much half of Uruguay in the form of the Blanco Party and be able to use Oribe's connections to the Argentine Federal Party in our favor as well.
Republic of Entre Rios
Entrerriano

The situation in Mesopotamia has gotten out of control. Last month the Governor of Corrientes was deposed by the Federalist guerrillas and the Governor of Entre Rios was charged with retaking Corrientes. Sadly, the governor's campaign was short-lived, since as soon as he left, the Federalists took Entre Rios capital and soon the governor would be defeat in battle, having to flee towards Buenos Aires. 

The Federalists have now declared the Republic of Entre Rios, intent on resisting Unitarian rule and establishing a Federalist government.

\n\nRecently we have been losing our grip over the Interior. With the region being far away from our power base and the local garrisons being weak and unruly, slowly our trusted governors were toppled by Federalist caudillos. Now, with the Federalists emboldened by their victories, they have finally organized themselves into the "Federal League of the Interior" — a Federalist confederation of the provinces in the Interior. With our hopes of the situation sorting itself dying out, it is about time we take action against this Federalist abomination before they set their eyes in our direction!

After a bloody campaign against the Colorado Party and their revolutionary allies in the Uruguayan countryside and a gruesome siege in Montevideo, we finally have defeated Rivera and his lackeys. Now we sit at the peace table having to decide the fate of Uruguay. 

We are meant to restore Oribe as president and have him as our faithful ally in Uruguay, but, considering we hold all the cards in this situation, it may be wise to sort out some border disputes as well, or even backstab Oribe and restore the Province of Cisplatina.

Platine dominance was a huge incentive to our invasion of Uruguay, and this fact wasn't lost on the Argentinians. Not only did the Unitarians were allied to the Farroupilha revolutionaries as well, but they also were naturally aligned to the Colorado Party's ideals, leading for them join their fight against us. Now that we decided the fate of Uruguay, the fragile Unitarian regime also waits on our veredict. 

During the war we enjoyed the support of the Federalist Party, which expects us to put them in charge of Argentina and, while we would be more than happy to do that, we also could guarantee our control over the La Plata Basin by taking over Argentine Mesopotamia, as a payment for our help, of course...

\n\nDespite Brazil's best efforts, we managed to—surprisingly—inflict a series of devastating defeats against Brazil and Oribe. Now, with the Brazilian armies defeated, our enemies have finally agreed to accept our terms for peace. While those terms are yet to be defined, one thing we want for certain is our contested territories and, beyond that, maybe liberating Rio Grande do Sul and Santa Catarina, maybe even uniting our three counties into the Uruguayan Federation...

\n\nDespite Brazil's best efforts, we managed to—surprisingly—inflict a series of devastating defeats against Brazil and Oribe. Now, with the Brazilian armies defeated, our enemies have finally agreed to accept our terms for peace. While those terms are yet to be defined, one thing we want for certain is our contested territories and, beyond that, maybe liberating Rio Grande do Sul and Santa Catarina, maybe even turning Rio Grande do Sul into the Province of San Pedro, keeping Santa Catarina as buffer...
Unitarian-Colorado Brotherhood

§RGain 2.5 infamy§! With the war against Brazil, a certain sense of brotherhood was created between the Unitarian Party and the Colorado Party. Despite both parties having their differences, after the war such differences seem small in comparison to their shared enemies. Not only that, but many Colorados ended up becoming more "Unitarian-minded" during the war. Maybe it would be a good idea to use our connections in the Colorado Party to put forward the union between Uruguay and Argentina. That would mean, of course, betraying Rivera and his republic, but that would be a small price to pay for Platinean Unity...

\n\nIn what started as a limited military campaign to support Chile in breaking the Peru-Bolivia Confederation slowly transformed into a successful invasion of Bolivia and South Peru. The Army of the North has completely beaten the Santa Cruz's confederation, occupying key cities such as Charcas, La Paz and the Confederation's constituent capital—Tacna. Despite our initial plans being limiting to just dissolving the Peru-Bolivian Confederation, maybe we could push for reintegration of Bolivia and maybe even control over South Peru, with some diplomatic insistence...

\n\nWith the Unitarians unable to defeat the rebellions across the Republic, we have finally seized Buenos Aires and assumed control of the government. Meeting with other Federalists in the country. The Argentine Republic was abolished and replaced by the Federalist-led Argentine Confederation.
Federalists Defeated

After a terrible campaign against the Federalists, we finally have put an end to any hope of a Federalist come back. Both the Interior and the Mesopotamia—historical Federalist strongholds—are now firmly under our rule. 

With the Federalist Threat gone, much of the fearmonger carried out by hardliners in the party is gone and we can finally think about establishing a definitive constitution for Argentina...
The Kandahar Expedition

§RGain 4 infamy§! With our rule over Herat consolidated and Britain out of the way for now, we are finally ready to shift our attention towards our next objective: The Emirate of Kandahar. The rulers of Kandahar, while appearing subservient to the Peacock Throne, having recently supported us in sieging Herat in exchange for the border province of Farah and Sabzevar, enjoys far too much autonomy and free movement to be considered a proper subject of Iran. \nKandahar is not simply a dependency of Iran, but an integral part of our domain and it is time to establish this fact. Let us send a full expedition to Kandahar and demand the total submission of the Emir.

\n\nWhile nominally a dependency of the Qajar Empire to the west, it seems such empty shows of submission could only stave off the Persians for so long as our de-facto independence from the Peacock Throne has now come under threat. We have received news of an emissary from Tehran arriving in Kandahar demanding a meeting with the Emir. Knowing it would be an urgent matter, we arranged an audience between the emissary and the Emir. Sadly, the only thing the emissary brought with him were threats of war: The Shah has demanded our submission to Tehran or face the same fate as the recently conquered Emirate of Herat. While such a show of arrogance is far from unexpected from the Persians and honour dictates we meet this grave insult on the field of battle, perhaps we could use this opportunity by offering our vassalage to the Shah in exchange for full control over the Emirate of Herat, as was once discussed briefly during the siege of Herat.

Our emissary has returned from Kandahar with interesting news. The Emir of Kandahar has presented us with a counteroffer—he will submit entirely to us so long as we grant him governorship of Herat, as was briefly discussed during the recent siege of the city. 

While usually we wouldn't be open to negotiating, the Emir of Kandahar proven a loyal ally. Granting him control over Herat would strengthen a local ally in the region while sparing valuable manpower and resources that could be better spent on other frontiers.

\n\nOur emissary hsa returned from Kandahar with unsurprising news. The Emir of Kandahar has refused to submit to us. Such an act of defiance hardly suprises us coming from such a volatile and savage region, even if it is from an old ally. We have no choice but to carry on with the expedition to Kandahar, lest the World know our words carry no weight.

The Khozeimeh Emirs of the Qa'inat have proven effective rulers of our eastern frontiers, both developing the harsh but valuable land and fending off Afghan and Turkmen raids. As our border continues to push east into Sistan and the fertile lands along the Helmand river, it would be wise to grant this land to the Emirs of the Qa'inat so that they may bring similar prosperity to the region.

The Emirate of Muhammarah has been an effective buffer in the vunerable flatlands of Arabestan, reducing friction with the local Arab population. As our border continues to push west into the marshlands of the Iraqi Delta, it would be wise to grant this land to the Emirs of the Arabestan so that they may bring effective local rule in our stead.

With our relentless push east into Makran, Herat, and Kandahar complete, we have reaffirmed Qajar control over the traditional Eastern frontiers of Iran. While our ambitions in the East don't end here, beyond Kandahar and Makran sit the imposing Sulaiman and Sefid-kuh Mountain Ranges, lands which, while productive, will prove difficult to fully control. 

As the dust settles, our new governors slowly settle in their new holdings. To the North, we see to the integration of the Pashtun and Hazara of Afghanistan and to the South, we strive to stake a claim over Kalat and Bela, to secure our dominion over Baluchistan.

\n\nOur emissary returned from Kandahar with welcome news. The Emir of Kandahar has accepted our demand submission, proving his loyalty to our Shah. Considering his past loyalty, we can expect him to not give us nearly as much trouble as the Emir of Herat did...
An Iranian Dynasty
Reaffirm our Claims over Bahrain

§RGain 2.5 infamy§! Our claim over the Bahrain Archipelago has always been complicated. With its population being mostly of Shiite-Iranian origin, we have traditionally ruled the area in an indirect manner, through the al-Mazkur Sheikhs of Bushehr. While we still hold a traditional claim to these islands, they haven't been under direct Iranian control since the Zand Dynasty, with Manamah currently ruled by the Sunni Banu Utub dynasty of Nejddi origin who have refused to reconize any kind of rule from Tehran, going as far as getting protection from the British to keep themselves free from our dominion. \n\nWhile we previously managed to strike an agreement with the British for the reincorporation of Bahrain into our state, the vile Europeans went back on their word, refusing to return Bahrain to us. It is about time we reaffirm our claims to Bahrain, with no regard to what the British or any other foreign power thinks about it!
Dissolve The Emirate of Kandahar

Until recently, Kandahar's position as an autonomous feudal entity has been an acceptable state of affair since their vassalization by Mohammad Shah. Now however, with gradual transformation of Iran from a feudal system to a proper nation state, the time has come to strip the Barakzai Emir in Kandahar of formal power and centralize our rule in the region, even if it may lead to friction with the local Afghans.
Dissolve The Emirate of Herat

Despite being counted amongst the core provinces of Iranian Khorasan in the days of the Safavid Shah Ismail, ever since the collapse of Nader Shah Afshar's Empire and the subsequent rise of ours, Herat has remained an independent thorn in our side despite numerous attempts to retake it. With our most recent attempt at achieving dominance over Herat successful, we are now in a position to assert more direct rule over Herat by deposing the Sadozai Emir and dissolving the Emirate entirely.
Restore Order to Maymana

§RGain 1 infamy§! Less than 300km from Herat lies the city of Maymana, an important center of commerce in the region and our gateway to Turkestan. The city—mostly inhabited by Uzbeks these days—sits at the middle of the Chahar Vilayets, a region inhabited by 4 khanates, Sar-i Pul, Sheberghan, Andkhui and—the most powerful and influential of them all—Maymana. Despite that, the Chahar Vilayets as a whole were far from stable, with the 4 khanates finding themselves at war with each other from time to time, not to mention when foreign powers such as Herat and Kabul having long coveted the region and especially Maymana—reconizing its importance in the region and the potential to control the rest of the khanates through the city. Now that Herat is firmly annexed into our nation, it is about time we bring order to the Chahar Vilayets by invading Maymana, securing our borders in the region and assuring our oversight over the region and its remaining khans!
Deal with the Turkmen Raids

The Turkmen and Uzbek Tribes have long been raiding our our northern territory, pillaging and taking some of our people captive to be sold as slaves. Many of these tribes act on the interests of the Khan of Khiva and the Emir of Bukhara, both of which profit greatly of the influx of slaves and riches captured by these raids. To deal with those troublesome tribals, we will have to first deal with Khiva and Bukhara—to leave the Turkmen and Uzbek tribes helpless against our advances and maybe even being able to end the trading of Iranian slaves in Khiva.

Our profiteering from the Turkmen and Uzbek raids against Northern Iran hasn't gone unnoticed in the eyes of the Shah. We have recieved news of the the Iranians have decided to act against us to end our indirect support to the their enemies and our enslavement of their people. We are left no option if not to show the Shah we can't be pushed around.

§RIf we lose this war, Iran will force us to end slavery and take control over Kyzyl-Su, Zysyl-Arbat, Merv and Üçajy!§!
Pacify Turkmenistan

With we being successful in putting an end to the raids that plagued our northern border, the Turkmen tribes that once relied on these raids have grown weak. If we were to better develop our administration, we could pacify one and for all those weakened tribes before our neighbors to the north can take advantage of them in our place.

\n\nAfter a series of decisive victories against the Uzbeks we are finally able to reach Khiva, invading the city and ending its status as a major slave market in the region. To guarantee that the city won't ever recover its position as a trade hub for buying and selling slaves we will keep a small garrison in the city, keeping an eye on the city and its traders.

Herat, Kandahar and Maymara... 

These three cities were once the center of power for mighty polities in our region, but now all of them have been subjugated under the Shah of Iran, raising the suspicions of the remaining khans of the region. While we always struggled to retain our independence, now it is clear to us that—if the Shah was to turn his eye towards us—we wouldn't be able to resist an invasion from the West. 

It would be in our best interest to guarantee any semblance of autonomy by asking for the protection of Kabul or Bukhara, with both its emirs having long displayed interest in our lands and would be happy to having us under their overlordship.

\n\nFollowing the Iranian annexation of Herat, Kandahar and Maymara, the Khan of $FROMCOUNTRY$ came to us promissing us their vassalage as long as we promise to protect them from Iran. Naturally, we wouldn't refuse such an offer, especially considering the Iranians present a threat to us as well.
Dominate Little Turkestan and Hazarajat

§RGain 5 infamy§! The region around Balkh—also known as Little Turkestan—has long been a contested between Iranian and Central Asian powers, with control over it shifting constantly back in the days of the Safavid Dynasty. The region nowdays is mostly contested between the Emirs of Bukhara and Kabul, with both having recently gained a stronger foothold in the region with local khans pledging allegiance to them as a to guarantee some sort of protection against us. While some of our advisors aren't sure about expanding eastwards, the truth is not doing so would be dooming the region to being dominated by either Kabul or Bukhara. Taking over the region—currently inhabited by Uzbeks—would not only deprive our rivals of useful land, but also isolate them from one another so we can take them out one at a time. \n\n§RYou need at least 75% civilization progress, the tooltip lies.§W
Take Control of the Amu Darya

§RGain 7.5 infamy§! With our successful campaigns in Balkh and Kunduz, fraying the local patchwork Warlords and Khans, we now hold control over the upper Amu Darya, a great river serving as the lifeblood of Central Asia's great settlements, including the city of Khiva. While it seems obvious to move down-stream to assert our control over the river's entire length, exerting control over the lands past the right bank of the river would prove too much for our nation's administration. Instead, we could subjugate the Emirate of Bukhara as a representative of the Shah's dominion over Transoxiana, a once mighty polity with a long and storied history of ruling the region that would help us assert control over the great Silk Road cities of Shahrisabz, Qarshi, Samarkand and Bukhara itself. 

We have already butted heads with Bukhara once already our success there assures us that they will be no match to our great state! 

§RYou need at least 80% civilization progress, the tooltip lies.§W

After a swift campaign against Bukhara, we have successfully subjugated their Emir, deposing him in favor of a more favorable member of his family, which is more than happy to act as the representative of the Shah's dominion over Transoxiana, securing our position on the eastern bank of the Amu Darya. 

Cisoxiana—the western bank of the Amu Darya—will be held under our direct control for the forseable future, with our annexation of Khiva collapsing the local khanate. The lands beyond the river—around Shimbay—being taken over by the Karakalpaks, with the region being too far for Bukhara to exert control over, with the Karakalpaks being free to enjoy their freedom while we consolidate our rule over our new gains.
Consolidate our Dominion over Transoxiana

With our administration modernized and control over Transoxiana and the Emirate of Bukhara pacified, it is about time we finally cross the Amu Darya and end Bukhara's autonomy before they become a problem for us, opening way for us to further influence Central Asia. \n\nAlso, we will take the opportunity to finally put an end to the pitiful khanate ruled by the Karakalpaks across the river from Khiva!

\n\nWhen the Second Treaty of Erzurum, there was an expectation by the Ottoman Government and international observers that there would be peace between the Ottoman Empire and the Iran. Now, with our claiming of Ottoman land, it becase clear that not only do we have little regard for peace in the region, but also for the international treaties that we sign, leading to minor condemnations by foreign powers interested in the region.

The First Treaty of Erzurum signed in 1823 came after the victory of Persia over the Ottoman Empire. Surprisingly, Persia asked only for continuation of being the protector of the Shias in the Ottoman Empire. While at the time no direct territorial demands were made, since then the Persian forces did push westward, claiming all of Iraq and a good portion of Kurdistan, pushing us back into Anatalia. 

Having been beaten and defeated by Persia again and again, the Persian government came to us with a proposal to meet in the small city of Van to sort out our overlapping claims in Eastern Anatolia. It is clear that they plan on pressuring us to drop all of the claims in their newly-conquered lands. While it would be hard to admit our repeated defeat at the hands of Persia, it is clear that we securing a more permanent peace with the rising Persia would be in our best interest...
\n\nThe Turks have accepted the treaty drafted by our diplomats in Van—peace has returned to our borders, and nothing will be able to change that!

\n\nThe Turks have refused to sign the treaty drafted by our diplomats in Van—we can no longer be so sure about peace in the region, as they may be just waiting for the opportunity to retake their lost territories.
Propose the Treaty of Van

With our advances into Mesopotamia and Kurdistan we have promptly usurped the Ottoman position as the overlords Mesopotamia and Eastern Anatolia, leading to our annexation of cities such as Baghdad, Mosul and Erzurum. Now, having defeated the Turks once again, we are in a position to pressure the Turks into signing a treaty to ratify the current border as permanent, to put and end to the hostilities between our nations. \n\nOf course, that would imply that we don't want to expand further into ther holdings, but that is a small price to pay for not having the risk of them striking us at our moment of weakness.
Syr Darya-Ferghana Line

§RGain 5 infamy§!Now that we have established our dominion over Transoxiana, it is hard to say that our ambitions in the region are satisfied. Instead, we have to note that, while we managed to secure our hold over Bukhara and Samarkand, those cities are hard to defend, considering how far they are from our holdings in Iran. Counterintuitively, the way to solve this would be to push further into Central Asia, dominating the Sart-dominated Urban centers of Tashkent and Kokand, claiming a line from the Ferghana Valley to the Syr Darya.

We have finally managed to revoke Muscat's lease over Oman. The arrangement with the Sultan of Muscat was profitable at first, but, with our ongoing instability and the lack of a naval force, the Muscat saw fit to not pay its stipends for the lease of the port—much to the dismay of our coffers, thinking that we could never contest their hold over the seas and that we wouldn't risk being punished for taking the port by force, but it seems that their naval advantage couldn't delay the inevitable forever, with the port finally returning to our hands. 

While the plan was to—at first—just seize the Sultan's assets in the city, it became dauntlessly obvious to us that Bandar Abbas is still a bigger and more modern port than Bushehr. Maybe it would be a good idea to once again turn Bandar Abbas into our main trading port, letting Bushehr be sidelined in favor of it.
The Kurdistan Offer

§RGain 5 infamy§! With the Crimean War raging on, it has become clear that the Western powers are plotting to undermine our position on the Concert of Europe! Starved out of our conventional allies in Europe, it becomes clear that to tip the war in our favor we need to resort to an unconventional player: The Qajars of Persia! \n\nWere we to offer the Qajars parts of Kurdistan in exchange for their joining our war against the Turks, opening a new front along the rugged Zagros mountain range to the Ottoman's east, distracting the Franco-Turkish armies and securing our victory in the war!

In the midst of a large war in Europe, the Russians, at war with the Turks, British, and French, have come to us with an enticing offer: We are invited to join their war against the Ottomans, opening a new front on the Turk's eastern flanks and, in exchange, the Russians will award us the territories comprising Kurdistan in the ensuing peace deal. 

§RDespite Russian promises, if we are unable to defeat the Ottomans before the Russians are victorious, they will not hesitate to only grant us a small piece of the promised reward!§W

The Persians, in the face of our generous offer, have refused our call to arms, most likely intimidated by the combined might of the Franco-Ottoman army and British navy! 

 While their betrayal was unexpected, we do not require their assistance in order to achieve victory in this conflict! 
Right?

\n\nThe Persians have accepted our offer to join the Crimean War! Declaring war on the Ottomans and their alliance and laying claim to Ottoman Kurdistan in the process! As their armies cross the border into Kurdish territory, it has become clear that the war has escalated far beyond a regional war around Black Sea -for better or for worse...

Despite our stalled advance on the Kurdish front, our Russian ally have managed to capitalize on the divertion created by our offensive, achieving victory in the war! 

That is not to say that all is well, though. The Russians have expressed contempt for our lackluster performance in the war, a sentiment shared by the other signatories of the Treaty of Paris, which have refused to grant us the full extent of our claims, severely limiting our gains!

Our role in the Crimean War has proven decisive! After a hard-fought campaign across the Zagros, we have inflicted devastating blows to the anti-Russian alliance, bleeding their armies out in the Armenian Highlands and securing our victory in the region! 

Naturally, we have been invited to Paris for the negotiations of the peace treaty. With the Russians pleased with our success and the defeated Europeans unable to raise objections, we have been awarded the entirety of our claims in Ottoman Kurdistan. Victory for the Shahanshah, long may he reign!

We have been dealt a decisive blow by the Persians... 

The Iranian front of the Crimean War has been a series of humiliating defeats for us. Now, as per the Treaty of Paris of $YEAR$, we are forced to cede them the totality of their claims in Kurdistan. 

This has effectively cut us off from our holdings in Mesopotamia, and as such forces us to grant additional autonomy to the area under a reformed Principality of Iraq to retain any semblance of rule in the region...

\n\nWith the Ottoman defeat in the Crimean War against the Russians and their Persian ally, the Qajar State has seized control over Ottoman Kurdistan, cutting the Turks off from their holdings in Iraq. They have since decided to place the region under the jurisdiction of "The Principality of Iraq" and, with our existing influence in the Persian Gulf, we can use this opportunity to secure some connections with the new Iraqi ruler to our benefit...
Orchestrate a coup against the Prince of Iraq

§RGain 5 infamy§! We have exercised a great deal of influence in Iraq since the Turkish defeat in the Crimean War, which led to the region entering a period of de-facto independence. With our growing role and interest in Mesopotamian politics, it is clear that, if we are to keep our investments safe from regional interference, we need to exert more direct control over the region. The $COUNTRY_ADJ$ resident in Baghdad has spend the last few months negotiating with the Prince of Iraq for the principality to accept protectorate status under our empire -to no avail. This leaves up no choice but to orchestrate a coup in the Iraqi Principality and ensure Baghdad is to be ruled by someone more willing to cooperate with us...
Enforce the Russian Demands

§RGain 8 infamy§! With our decisive victory over the Ottomans we have forced the Turks onto the negotiating table and are free to enforce our promised claims in Kurdistan, greatly assisting our Russian allies in the war!

\n\nIn the past, we have imposed the treaties of Gulistan and Turkmenchay on the Qajar Dynasty in Iran. These defeats at our hands cemented Iran's position as a playground for our empire, with out government considering Iran to be in our sphere of influence for a long while after the Treaty to Turkmenchay. Sadly, as Iran modernized and rose once again to proeminence, it seems that the Iranians haven't forgotten what we took from them  following their defeat at our hands. Now, Iran, as a great power in the Middle East, turns its attention to revoking the Treaties of Gulistan and Turkmenchay, declaring war on us for the their lands in the Caucasus.

\n\nAfter a series of victories, we have forced the Russians into the negotiating table, drafting the peace treaty in the Caspian city of Baku. While some of our advisors try to push for a limited peace settlement—restricting the treaty to the return of the lands of the Erivan, Nakhchivan and Talysh Khanates, lost in 1828. Of course, most of our envoys to Baku support the reclaiming of all of our lost territory, including Armenia, Azerbaijan and Eastern Georgia—even if we would need to pressure Russia quite a bit to get our hands on the latter. A mora extreme approach would be to seize all of our claims up to Dagestan, while expelling Russia from the Southern Caucasus by liberating Western Georgia under a new, semi-independent Georgian Kingdom, ruled by one of the Imereti branches of the Bagrotioni Dynasty.

Unsurprisingly, this little display of defiance from the Persians achieved little besides giving us a reason to remind them of their place beneath us. After a grueling campaign in Iran, we have finally subdued our enemies, forcing them to negotiate with us in Tabriz. 

As we draft a peace deal, two options emerge—a moderate and a radical one. The moderate option is take a small strip of land along our border and the Caspian coast, letting us connect our Caucasian holds with Turkmenistan through the south. The other, more radical option, relies on us convening with Britain to ensure that Iran knows their place and never again tries to defy an European power. We would seize major swathes of land together with Britain, while keeping Iran as a buffer between our empires—of course...

\n\nWe have been keeping a close eye on the war between Russia and Persia for the last few months. We considered sending Persia some support, but we many in our government were unwilling to back Persia in a war that they themselves started. Unsurprisingly, the Russians have occupied Tehran and the Persians have surrended. Soon we would be surprised with a Russian delegation inviting us to negotiate the carving of Persia with them. While we can deny Russia's offer, considering they already annexed most of Northern Persia, it seems that we only stand to gain from annexing some of the Persian territory.

\n\nThe Anglos have accepted to sign the Anglo-Russian Convention of $YEAR$ on Persia, carving up Persia between us two. Persia now has been taught to never again mess with us, or face even more humiliation.
\n\nThe Anglos have refused to sign the Anglo-Russian Convention on Persia, refusing to take part in our occupation of Iran and ilegitimizing our actions in the process.

\n\nRussia has decided to leave it clear to us that we are nothing more than the playground of foreign empires, calling up Britain to humiliate us further and transform us into a buffer between our their empires...

\n\nDespite sorting out our position in the Caucasus, there is still one matter to be resolved in Baku. Ever since we started to expand into Turkestan we came into contact with the Russian forts and caravans in the region. At first, our interactions in the region were surprisingly amicable, but as time went on we slowly began to clash with the Russians, which played a part in our decision to denounce our treaties with Russia. As of now hold enough negotiating power to solve this matter once and for all, by demanding the total withdraw of Russia from Central Asia, potentially turning the region into the our diplomatic backyard.

\n\nDespite their gains in the Caucasus, the Persian Lion is hardly done humiliating us! On top of withdrawing from their claims in the Caucasus, the Persians have also forced us to leave Central Asia all together, forcing us to leave any of our forts south of Orenburg, essentially securing Central Asia as part of Persia's sphere of influence.

\n\nThe Gate of the Alans—known in our language as Dar-i Alan—is a vital chasm right in the middle of the Caucacus Mountains that works as basically a major crossing between both sides of the Caucasus. The "Gate" is part of the Georgian Military Road, a route on which Russian troups went through to cross from the heartlands of Russia into Transcaucasia and was severely improved under Russian ownership of the Causcasus. While the Russian infrastructure on the route is impressive, we have little use for it now that we annexed most of Transcaucasia, with the road slowly became less and less important—turning into just another border crossing for us to guard. As a result—to save ourselves the trouble of maintaining the road and guarding the "Gate of the Alans"—many of our advisors have suggested that we should grant ownership of the road to our Georgian subjects, while keeping Tbilisi for ourselves—of course....

Regnum Sardiniae et Corsicae

§RGain 5 infamy§! The Regnum Sardiniae et Corsicae was established by Pope Boniface VIII, who granted the title to James II of Aragon, turning him into the nominal King of Sardinia and Corsica in 1297. Despite that, James II would only start to conquer Sardinia in 1324 and Aragonese rule over the island would be contested until 1420. Not only that, but Aragon would never manage to encroach in the Island of Corsica, letting the inland remain under Genoese and then French control, despite a brief period of independence. \n\nNow, more than 500 years since the kingdom's creation, it is about time time we reclaim ownership over the Kingdom of Sardinia and Corsica and, for the first time, truly unify both islands under our rule.
\n\nWe have been forced to expatriate the Bourbons. Sadly, we are now obligated to lead our kin elsewhere, well away from the French lands they once ruled.
\n\nWe have been beaten back the French, preventing them for forcefully expatriating the French Bourbons. They will reside in our realm whether they like it or not!

\n\nAfter a bit of force, we forced the crown in Spain to back down and exile the French Bourbons from their country. Political security has been secured, and the French Bourbons have been forced to move elsewhere, hopefully somewhere further away from the border.

\n\nThe Crown of Spain has just reaffirmed—through iron and blood—that we can't violate its sovereignty to keep the Bourbon monarchs within their borders, despite our attempts to expatriate them by force. We have little recourse in this matter, save dropping the matter entirely.

\n\nAfter failing to expatriate the Charlex X and his descendents from Spain, we would soon found ourselves at war with Spain, leading to our regretable defeat not long after. The Spanish made sure to take a piece of our core territory in the peace deal, a decision that baffled all but the most insane of observers, but soon the reason would become clear. The territory annexed by Spain became, in practive, the a Bourbon fief, with the descendents of Charles X transforming it into a little Bourbon France. Now, this folly of a king has declared independence from Spain and state his desire to retake his rightful throne, marching on our territory. Now we have no choice but to put an end to the Bourbon menace once and for all.

\n\nThe idea of moving the nation's capital to Saint Louis isn't a new one. Such an idea has been floating around since the Mexican-American War, with some people seeing the city's central location as perfect for the expanding American nation. After the American Civil War, there were at least three proposed resolutions to move the capital to St. Louis, instead of renovating the expanding District of Columbia. Only the third proposal, by Herbert Paine, as a joke, managed to be put to a vote, where it was defeated by a vote of 77 to 97. After that, the idea was still pushed around by a few minds in the Midwest, but it would ultimately die down not long after.
William I's Ambitions

§RGain 15 infamy§! While the Congress of Vienna saw us grow with the annexation of the Austrian Netherlands and personal union over Luxemburg, William I had the ambition of getting pretty much most of what is now the Prussian Rhineland, connecting the Netherlands to his hereditary lands in Nassau. His ambitions, however, would be set aside, as Prussia as offered the Rhineland and the Netherlands was left at its current size, despite some resistance from William I. Now, with our position as a great power safe, we can consider if reigniting these old ambitions would be worth, considering that we would not only be upseting the balance of power established by the Congress of Vienna, but also poking the hornet's nest that is Germany.
Is Iran
Reaffirm Claims on Bahrain

§RGain 2.5 infamy§! The Bahrain Archipelago was always traditionally tied to Iran, with its population being mostly of Iranian origin, with Iran usually ruling it indirectly, through its emirs in the port of Bushehr. While the Qajars still hold claims to these islands, they haven't been under Iranian overlordship ever since the Zand Dynasty, with the island being ruled by Sunni emirs that have since refused to reconize rule from Tehran, going as far as getting protection from the British to keep themselves free from our rule. \n\nWhile we managed to strike an agreement with the British for the reincorporation of Bahrain, the vile Europeans went back on their word, refusing to return Bahrain to us. It is about time we reaffirm our claims to Bahrain, with no regard to what the British or any other foreign power thinks about it!

Once a center of German culture and politics, now the streets of Frankfurt are lay haunted by gunfire as the French soldiers quell what little resistance is left to their occupation of the city. The $FROMCOUNTRY_ADJ$ soldiers flood the Bundespalais, looking for the Austrian representative to the German Confederation, soon finding out that he has long made his way back to Austria. With the palace deserted, a $FROMCOUNTRY_ADJ$ officer would read orders coming from the $FROMCOUNTRY_ADJ$ capital ordering the burning of the Palais Thurn und Taxis. Slowly the fire would consume the bulding, spreading to the nearby buldings and leading to vivid flames to take over the Frankfurter skyline. 

The Burning of the Bundespalais would strike a great blow to the entire German Confederation, and by carrying out such a grievous act $FROMCOUNTRY$ challenges the Concert of Europe drawn by the diplomats at the Congress of Vienna. Now many of the German leaders sit uneasy not knowing what the future holds for the German world now that the fate of the German Confederation is uncertain.
Burn the Bundespalais

§RGain 5 infamy§! As the center of German politics, Frankfurt sits as one of the most important cities in the German Confederation, together with Berlin and Vienna. By capturing it we could take over the Palais Palais Thurn und Taxis—The Bundespalais—and strike a huge blow against the German world by burning it to the ground, pressing the German Confederation to come to the negotiation table. Doing so would naturally be seen as an challenge to the European order, but it may be worth it...

\n\nThe Bundespalais burns! Our representatives to the Federal Convention luckily made their way out of the city long before the invading troops reached the city, but that hardly means we are safe. The war still wages on and—with the failture of the German Confederation to protect its own capital—the German nationalists are once again gaining popularity, defending the end of the German Confederation and the creating of a German Empire to better find foreign threats and protect the German people.

\n\nAlthough $COUNTRY$ has tried to control the Italian peninsula dividing it between Savoy-Piedmont, Naples, and the Papal States, the fires of Italian nationalism continue to burn. Many in Paris are of the opinion that a unified Italy is impossible to avoid, and it is best that the Italian state be created peacefully through $COUNTRY_ADJ$ guidance rather through a bloody revolution. Others argued that a unified Italy will quickly grow out of our control. Either way a decision must be reached on what to do with Italy. The question then arises who shall rule the Italy. Should it be Savoyards in the north? Perhaps the House of Savoy rule in Turin will result in a loyal and capable subject? On the other hand, the rulers of Naples were once of French blood, and it could be wise to keep things in the family. There are some who say the crown should go to neither the Savoyards nor the Sicilians, but rather the Holy Father himself in Rome. And of course there are those who think the right course of action is to tighten our grip on Italy.

\n\nWith our recent treaties with the Gulf States, we managed to create an informal protectorate currently known as "The Trucial States". We hold a loose hand over the states in the Gulf, but the local $COUNTRY_ADJ$ resident in the region suggest we could deepen our relationship now that the Gulf States are completely under our diplomatic control, pushing for a closer relationship between us and them.
Reclaim the Kingdom of Westphalia

§RGain 5 infamy§! Sharing little to no territory with Westphalia proper, the (Napoleonic) Kingdom of Westphalia was established by Napoleon after the Peace of Tilsit in 1807, with it being carved from Prussian and to Napoleon's youngest brother, Jérôme Bonaparte. Westphalia was meant to be a model state to the other states of the Confederation of the Rhine and while it succeed at that to some degree, Jérôme's spending practices soon would alienate him towards his older brother. While the Kingdom of Westphalia was soon dissolved with Napoleon's defeat, now that Westphalia exists once again we are free to claim the territories that it had under the rule of Napoleon.

\n\nWith the French victory against Prussia in the Rhine Crisis and the rise of German nationalism as the Springtime of Nations rages on, the new Bavarian Minister-President, came to us with the Trias Plan, promoting the idea that we rally Baden and Württemberg to unite into a third major power in Germany, the South German Federation. Seeing as both Austria and Prussia failed to protect the Bundespalais and the Confederation as a whole, it seems like the only reasonable course of action is stepping up to protect our brothers, instead of being subject to the whims of Berlin and Vienna.

\n\nIn light of the recent events, Bavaria has invited us to unite with them in a federation of South German states to counterbalance Austro-Prussian influence in the region. With the recent wave of nationalist revolutions and the defeats at the hands of the French, this may be the safest cost of action to take.

\n\nThe German Confederation is dead. After the defeat at the hands of the French and the Burning of Frankfurt,the liberal revolutions that swept the country were too much for the system put in place by Metternich. The Prussians have rallied part of the Confederation around them, with the intent of protecting them against further French agression and eventually revenging the loss of the Rhineland and Frankfurt. Meanwhile closer to home, the Bavarians broke off from the Confederation by forming a federated state with Baden and Württemberg, trying to create a new major power in Germany, straying away from both Prussian and Austrian influence.
